topic: A person finds out their biography was written without their knowledge
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias traced the embossed lettering on the brittle parchment, the fine script mocking his bewildered gaze. Every carefully chosen anecdote, every celebrated triumph, felt like a meticulously constructed illusion. He sat by the window, the afternoon light illuminating the dust motes dancing in the air, and let the silence of the room settle around him. His fingers moved slowly across the paper, not in anger, but with a deliberate, almost meditative slowness. He took a deep, measured breath, the air cool against his skin, and forced his shoulders to drop an inch. He needed time to process the sheer audacity of it all, to let the shock recede from a frantic tremor in his chest. He would read it all, piece by painstaking piece, allowing the narrative to unfold before him, giving himself the space to absorb the deception.

[story 2]

"So, this is how they framed it," Clara murmured, her voice barely above a whisper as she reread the fabricated memoir. She leaned back in her worn armchair, the springs groaning in protest beneath her. Her hands rested lightly on her knees, palms upturned, a gesture of quiet acceptance. The ink seemed to swim before her eyes, blurring the lines between truth and fiction. She didn't rush. Instead, she focused on the texture of the paper, the faint scent of aged glue, allowing the sheer weight of the revelation to settle into her bones rather than erupting into a storm. A slow, steady rhythm took over her breathing, a deliberate attempt to keep the internal chaos from spilling out into clumsy outbursts.

[story 3]

He kept turning the pages, his movements economical and unhurried. The realization hit him not as a sudden jolt, but as a slow, pervasive chill that spread from his core outward. He didn't move from his seat, simply letting his gaze drift over the carefully curated life story. A faint, almost imperceptible tightening around his jaw was the only outward sign of the internal struggle. He picked up a pen, not to write, but just to hold the cool metal, watching the way the light caught the polished surface. He allowed the silence to stretch, letting the enormity of the situation wash over him, treating the discovery like a difficult, lengthy equation that required careful, unhurried calculation.
